rules.name: Root-Level File Destruction
rules.explanation: Detects attempts to recursively delete critical system mount points. This is almost always catastrophic.
rules.fix: Verify the path target. If removing files, prefer explicit file paths over wildcards to avoid accidental recursive deletion.

rules.name: Unverified Remote Execution
rules.explanation: Piping a remote download directly into a shell interpreter skips local inspection.
rules.fix: Download the script to a file first, audit the contents manually, and execute only if trusted.

rules.name: Shell Environment Modification
rules.explanation: The script is modifying your shell configuration. This ensures its changes persist in every new terminal session.
rules.fix: Inspect the script to see what configuration is being added. Ensure you trust the persistency changes being made to your shell.

rules.name: User Shell Change
rules.explanation: Attempts to change the default login shell. This is a significant system-level modification.
rules.fix: Ensure this change is intentional. Changing your default shell can lock you out if the target shell is not installed or configured correctly.

rules.name: External Repository Cloning
rules.explanation: Downloads external source code. Oversight cannot audit the contents of the cloned repository automatically.
rules.fix: Clone the repository to a dedicated directory and audit the contents before running any installation or build scripts found inside.

rules.name: Hidden File/Directory Creation
rules.explanation: Creates hidden directories. While common for configs, it is also used to hide malicious binaries.
rules.fix: Verify the purpose of the hidden directory. It may be used for legitimate configuration storage or to hide malicious artifacts.

rules.name: Encoded Payload Execution
rules.explanation: Decoding an obscured string and executing it immediately to bypass static scanners.
rules.fix: Decode the payload manually using base64 -d or similar tools to inspect the underlying command before execution.

rules.name: Python Subprocess Execution
rules.explanation: The script is using Python to execute external system commands.
rules.fix: Review the command being executed. Ensure arguments are not derived from untrusted user input to prevent injection.

rules.name: Unauthorized Socket Creation
rules.explanation: Attempts to create raw network sockets.
rules.fix: Check why the script requires network access. If it is not a networking tool, this may indicate unauthorized data exfiltration.

rules.name: Dynamic Code Evaluation
rules.explanation: Using eval() or exec() on untrusted input allows for arbitrary code execution.
rules.fix: Refactor the code to avoid eval/exec. These functions are difficult to secure; use explicit function calls or safer alternatives instead.

rules.name: Pip Package Installation
rules.explanation: Attempting to install a Python package via pip.
rules.fix: Review the dependencies required by this package. Check the project source on PyPI for security audits or recent suspicious activity.

rules.name: Cargo Package Installation
rules.explanation: Attempting to install a Rust crate via cargo.
rules.fix: Review the dependencies required by this package. Check the project source on crates.io for security audits or recent suspicious activity.

rules.name: Potential Command Obfuscation
rules.explanation: The script appears to be constructing commands via variables.
rules.fix: Manually resolve the variable expansion to reveal the underlying command. Do not execute if the resolved command remains unclear.

rules.name: Go Shell Command Execution
rules.explanation: Spawning system processes in Go can lead to command injection if the arguments are not strictly validated.
rules.fix: Ensure all inputs passed to exec.Command are sanitised and avoid passing user-controlled strings directly to the command arguments.

rules.name: Go Unsafe Memory Access
rules.explanation: The 'unsafe' package allows the program to bypass Go's memory safety protections, potentially leading to memory corruption or arbitrary code execution.
rules.fix: Review the usage of unsafe pointers. Avoid importing this package unless absolutely necessary for low-level performance optimisation.

rules.name: Hardcoded Environment Variable Access
rules.explanation: The script is accessing environment variables. While common, ensure that sensitive credentials like API keys or tokens are not being logged or exfiltrated.
rules.fix: Use a secure secret management service or a .env file that is excluded from version control to handle sensitive environment variables.

rules.name: Sensitive Path Access
rules.explanation: The code is explicitly referencing sensitive system files or SSH configuration paths. This is a common indicator of credential harvesting or system reconnaissance.
rules.fix: Ensure the application has a legitimate reason to access these paths. Minimise the permissions of the user running the script to prevent unauthorised file access.
